ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for 'cereal with fruits or nuts with milk' as a composite dish; closest defensible anchor is the cereal food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Breakfast cereals) used as the closest defensible category; no exact composite code for cereal-fruit-nut-milk mixture exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this composite cereal dish without risk of fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 200 g (one bowl), consisting of roughly 40–50 g of cereal with dried fruits or nuts topped with approximately 150 ml of plain milk.

nutrient_source.title: Cereal with fruits or nuts with milk — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05050012 for 'Cereal with fruits or nuts with milk'. All per-100g values preserved as reported. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Cereals, ready-to-eat, with raisins, nuts, and honey' combined with whole milk contribution, yielding approximately 1.2 g saturated fat per 100 g of composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for muesli-type breakfast cereal with milk
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI entry exists for 'cereal with fruits or nuts with milk' as a composite HPB dish. GI of 66 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for muesli-type breakfast cereal served with full-fat milk (GI range 55–70, representative value 66), consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (66 × 36.5 g carbohydrate in 200 g serving) / 100 = 24.1. This is classified as medium GI (56–69 range).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.2 g/100 g) with very low dietary fibre (0.87 g/100 g) and negligible potassium (8 mg/100 g), which provides no offset to sodium (98 mg/100 g); added sugar promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this cereal dish due to its high sugar-to-fibre ratio and negligible potassium; substituting with plain oats or unsweetened muesli with low-fat milk significantly reduces the glycaemic and sodium-retention burden.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-to-high glycaemic load per serving (GL 24.1) driven by 19.3 g carbohydrate/100 g and 9.2 g sugar/100 g with only 0.87 g dietary fibre/100 g, resulting in rapid postprandial glucose excursion.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this cereal dish; the high glycaemic load and low fibre content will cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation, and replacing it with plain rolled oats (GI ~55) with unsweetened milk and a small portion of whole nuts is strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat (~1.2 g/100 g from milk and cereal components) combined with high added sugar (9.2 g/100 g) and low dietary fibre (0.87 g/100 g) contributes to an unfavourable lipid and inflammatory profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption of this cereal dish; the combination of added sugar and saturated fat from milk raises LDL-cholesterol and triglyceride risk, and switching to unsweetened whole-grain cereal with plant-based milk would provide a more cardioprotective breakfast option.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar and refined grain base promote pro-inflammatory mediators; dietary fibre at 0.87 g/100 g is insufficient to support anti-inflammatory gut microbiome activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should replace this cereal dish with plain oats topped with walnuts and fresh berries to obtain genuine anti-inflammatory omega-3 and polyphenol benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cereal with Fruits or Nuts with Milk is a ready-to-eat breakfast dish consisting of commercially prepared breakfast cereal mixed with dried fruits (such as raisins or cranberries) or nuts (such as almonds or cashews), served with plain cow's milk. The dish requires no cooking and is assembled by combining the dry cereal-fruit-nut mixture with cold or warm milk in a bowl. The texture is a combination of crunchy cereal pieces softened by milk, chewy dried fruit, and firm nut pieces, with a mildly sweet flavour from both the cereal's added sugar and the natural sugars in the dried fruit. It is widely consumed in Singapore as a convenient breakfast or snack and is catalogued in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo, entry F05050012). Per 100 g, the dish provides 129 kcal, 4.03 g protein, 3.82 g fat, 19.33 g carbohydrate, 9.17 g sugar, 0.87 g dietary fibre, 98 mg sodium, and 122 mg calcium. The calcium content is notable due to the milk component. Clinically, the dish carries a medium-to-high glycaemic load (GL ~24 per 200 g serving, GI ~66) and is not recommended for individuals with type 2 diabetes or hypertension due to its high sugar content, low fibre, and negligible potassium. The saturated fat contribution from milk is moderate. For health-conscious consumers, substituting sweetened cereal with plain rolled oats or unsweetened muesli and using low-fat or plant-based milk substantially improves the nutritional profile.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05050012, which is an approved primary source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy as a secondary approved source. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database using the closest analogous muesli-type cereal with milk entry, which is the approved GI source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite cereal-fruit-nut-milk dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05050012, and no exact match exists in USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite preparation as of 2026. Saturated fat of 1.2 g/100 g is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 08013, cereals ready-to-eat with raisins nuts and honey) combined with whole milk contribution; this value should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis if available. Potassium value of 8 mg/100 g as reported by HPB is unusually low for a milk-containing dish and may reflect the specific cereal-to-milk ratio used in the HPB analysis; this value is preserved as reported from the approved source. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for muesli-type cereal with full-fat milk; no exact GI measurement for this specific HPB composite dish was found in the approved GI database. Dietary tags 'plant_based' from the input record have been removed as the dish contains cow's milk and is therefore not plant-based; 'low_calorie' and 'low_sodium' tags are retained as the dish is 129 kcal/100 g and 98 mg sodium/100 g.
